_comment: Curated library of 100 landmark environmental stories. Every entry links to a stable, real page (Wikipedia / WHO / NOAA) that opens in a new tab - no API key, no cost, links never rot. Served by services/news.py.

items.title: The sea that vanished: how the Aral Sea lost 90% of its water in one generation
items.description: Once the world's fourth-largest lake, the Aral Sea between Uzbekistan and Kazakhstan shrank to a tenth of its size after its rivers were diverted for cotton - widely called the worst environmental disaster of the 20th century.

items.title: Aralkum: the newest desert on Earth appeared where a sea used to be
items.description: The exposed seabed of the Aral Sea became a 60,000 km² desert whose toxic salt-dust storms travel hundreds of kilometres and damage crops and lungs across Central Asia.

items.title: 200,000 saiga antelopes dropped dead in three weeks in Central Asia
items.description: In May 2015 more than half of the world's entire saiga population died in Kazakhstan within days - a bacterial infection triggered by unusual heat and humidity killed whole herds where they stood.

items.title: Semipalatinsk: 456 nuclear tests next door to Central Asia's steppe
items.description: The Soviet Union detonated hundreds of nuclear devices at the Semipalatinsk site in Kazakhstan; radiation exposure affected an estimated 1.5 million people and the land remains scarred today.

items.title: The snow leopard: Central Asia's mountain ghost is down to a few thousand
items.description: Fewer than 7,000 snow leopards survive across the mountains of Central Asia, including ranges bordering Uzbekistan - poaching and shrinking prey have made sightings vanishingly rare.

items.title: The Caspian Sea is shrinking - and could lose a third of its area this century
items.description: The world's largest inland body of water is dropping by centimetres every year as inflows fall and evaporation rises, threatening fisheries, ports and unique species like the Caspian seal.

items.title: A dust storm turned Tashkent's air the worst in the world for a night
items.description: Dust storms - increasingly fed by degraded drylands and the dried Aral seabed - can multiply PM levels dozens of times in hours, exactly the kind of event air-quality monitoring exists to catch.

items.title: Air pollution kills about 7 million people every year
items.description: The WHO estimates ambient and household air pollution together cause around 7 million premature deaths annually - more than malaria, HIV and road accidents combined.

items.title: 99% of humanity breathes air that exceeds WHO pollution guidelines
items.description: Almost no one on Earth lives with air the WHO considers fully safe - and the highest exposures are concentrated in low- and middle-income countries.

items.title: The Great Smog of London killed up to 12,000 people in five days
items.description: In December 1952 coal smoke trapped by cold air turned London's streets black at noon; the death toll forced the world's first modern clean-air laws.

items.title: Donora, 1948: the smog that poisoned a whole American town
items.description: A temperature inversion trapped zinc-works fumes over Donora, Pennsylvania for five days - 20 people died, 6,000 fell ill, and US air regulation began.

items.title: Delhi's winter air routinely hits 20 times the safe pollution limit
items.description: Crop burning, traffic and temperature inversions push the Indian capital's PM2.5 into 'severe' territory every winter, closing schools for millions of children.

items.title: A 3-km-thick brown haze hangs over southern Asia every winter
items.description: The 'Asian brown cloud' - a seasonal layer of soot and aerosols visible from space - dims sunlight, disrupts monsoons and darkens Himalayan glaciers.

items.title: PM2.5: the invisible particles small enough to enter your bloodstream
items.description: Particles under 2.5 micrometres slip past the lungs' defences into the blood, driving heart attacks, strokes and dementia - the single metric behind most AQI warnings.

items.title: Cooking smoke kills millions: household air pollution's hidden toll
items.description: Around 2 billion people still cook with wood, dung or coal; the smoke in their own kitchens causes an estimated 3+ million deaths a year, mostly women and children.

items.title: Acid rain once dissolved forests and statues across two continents
items.description: Sulfur and nitrogen from coal plants turned rain acidic enough to kill lakes across Scandinavia and North America - then emissions controls largely fixed it, proving regulation works.

items.title: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch is three times the size of France
items.description: An estimated 1.8 trillion plastic pieces circulate in the North Pacific gyre - most of it fishing gear and fragments that break down into microplastics but never disappear.

items.title: Microplastics have been found in human blood, lungs and placentas
items.description: Particles from packaging, tyres and clothing now show up inside human bodies; scientists are still working out what that means for long-term health.

items.title: A truckload of plastic enters the ocean every minute
items.description: Roughly 8-11 million tonnes of plastic reach the sea each year; without change, plastic could outweigh fish in the ocean by 2050.

items.title: More than 120 countries have now banned or taxed plastic bags
items.description: From Bangladesh (first, in 2002) to the whole of the EU, single-use bag rules have become one of the fastest-spreading environmental laws in history.

items.title: The ocean is 30% more acidic than before the industrial era
items.description: Seawater absorbs a quarter of our CO₂ emissions, and the resulting acidification is dissolving the shells of the tiny creatures that feed everything else.

items.title: Half of the world's coral reefs have already been lost
items.description: Marine heatwaves force corals to expel the algae they live on - mass 'bleaching' events that used to be rare now strike the same reefs every few years.

items.title: The Great Barrier Reef has bleached five times in just eight years
items.description: The world's largest living structure - visible from space - suffered mass bleaching in 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022 and 2024 as ocean temperatures broke records.

items.title: Ocean dead zones have quadrupled since 1950
items.description: Fertiliser runoff feeds algae blooms that strip oxygen from the water; the Gulf of Mexico's summer dead zone alone can exceed 20,000 km² - larger than Israel.

items.title: The Citarum in Indonesia may be the most polluted river on Earth
items.description: Textile factories and waste from 25 million people turned the river's surface into a carpet of garbage; lead levels have measured 1,000 times US safety standards.

items.title: The Ganges: holy to a billion people, and one of the world's most polluted rivers
items.description: Sewage from hundreds of cities flows largely untreated into the river that 400 million people depend on - decades of cleanup campaigns have struggled to keep pace.

items.title: Flint, Michigan: a whole city was poisoned by its own tap water
items.description: A cost-saving switch of water source in 2014 leached lead from old pipes into the drinking water of 100,000 people - officials denied the problem for over a year.

items.title: Lake Chad has lost about 90% of its area since the 1960s
items.description: A lake that sustained 30 million people across four African countries has shrunk dramatically from irrigation and drought - a slow-motion crisis echoing the Aral Sea.

items.title: The Colorado River usually no longer reaches the sea
items.description: So much water is withdrawn for 40 million people and 5.5 million acres of farmland that one of North America's great rivers typically runs dry before its delta.

items.title: America's breadbasket is draining an aquifer that took 10,000 years to fill
items.description: The Ogallala Aquifer under the US Great Plains is being pumped far faster than it recharges; parts of it could be effectively exhausted within decades.

items.title: Day Zero: the modern city that nearly ran out of water
items.description: In 2018 Cape Town came within weeks of shutting off taps for 4 million people; only drastic rationing - and rain - averted the first 'Day Zero' of a major city.

items.title: Chernobyl: the accident that made a region uninhabitable for centuries
items.description: The 1986 reactor explosion spread radioactive fallout across Europe and created a 2,600 km² exclusion zone that remains largely empty of people to this day.

items.title: Fukushima: a tsunami triggered the worst nuclear accident since Chernobyl
items.description: The 2011 earthquake and tsunami caused three reactor meltdowns in Japan and forced over 150,000 people from their homes; decommissioning will take decades.

items.title: Bhopal: the deadliest industrial disaster in history
items.description: A 1984 gas leak at a pesticide plant in India exposed half a million people to methyl isocyanate overnight; estimates of the eventual death toll reach into the tens of thousands.

items.title: Deepwater Horizon poured oil into the Gulf of Mexico for 87 days straight
items.description: The 2010 blowout released about 4.9 million barrels of oil - the largest marine oil spill ever - and its effects on deep-sea life are still being measured.

items.title: The Exxon Valdez spill still stains Alaskan beaches - dig and you'll find oil
items.description: Thirty-five years after the 1989 tanker disaster, pockets of oil persist under Prince William Sound's beaches, and some wildlife populations never fully recovered.

items.title: Minamata: the town poisoned by the fish it ate
items.description: A chemical factory discharged mercury into a Japanese bay for decades; thousands suffered severe neurological disease, and the case defined industrial pollution law worldwide.

items.title: Love Canal: the neighbourhood built on 21,000 tonnes of toxic waste
items.description: An entire New York suburb - including a school - was built over a chemical dump; the 1978 evacuation created the US Superfund toxic-cleanup programme.

items.title: Kyshtym, 1957: the nuclear disaster the world didn't hear about for 20 years
items.description: A waste-tank explosion at a secret Soviet plutonium plant contaminated 20,000 km² - kept classified so long that maps simply omitted the villages that were erased.

items.title: Lake Karachay: an hour on its shore was once a lethal dose
items.description: Used as a dumping ground for Soviet nuclear waste, this small Urals lake became arguably the most radioactively contaminated open-air spot on the planet; it has since been filled in with concrete.

items.title: A mine in Papua New Guinea dumped 80,000 tonnes of waste into a river - every day
items.description: The Ok Tedi mine discharged tailings directly into the river system for decades, degrading 1,500 km² of forest and the livelihoods of 50,000 downstream villagers.

items.title: The Niger Delta has suffered the equivalent of an Exxon Valdez spill every year for 50 years
items.description: Thousands of pipeline leaks have made parts of the delta one of the most oil-polluted places on Earth, with drinking-water benzene at 900 times WHO guidelines in places.

items.title: Seveso: the dioxin cloud that changed European industrial law
items.description: A 1976 reactor burst in Italy exposed a town to TCDD, one of the most toxic substances known - the EU's major-accident directives are literally named after it.

items.title: The Amazon has lost an area of forest larger than France since 1970
items.description: Around 17-20% of the world's largest rainforest is gone, and scientists warn parts of it are approaching a tipping point where forest flips to savanna.

items.title: Borneo has lost half its rainforest in a single human lifetime
items.description: Logging and oil-palm expansion cleared over 30 million hectares; the island's orangutans, pygmy elephants and clouded leopards are squeezed into shrinking fragments.

items.title: Palm oil is in half of supermarket products - and it cost Southeast Asia its forests
items.description: The world's most-used vegetable oil drove some of the fastest deforestation ever recorded; certification schemes now race to make the crop traceable.

items.title: Bornean orangutan numbers halved in 60 years
items.description: More than 100,000 orangutans disappeared from Borneo between 1999 and 2015 alone - one of the steepest declines ever measured for a great ape.

items.title: Australia's Black Summer: 3 billion animals killed or displaced
items.description: The 2019-20 bushfires burned 24+ million hectares, turned Sydney's air hazardous for weeks and produced smoke that circled the entire globe.

items.title: Canadian wildfire smoke turned New York's sky orange - 600 km away
items.description: The record 2023 fire season burned 18 million hectares and pushed New York City's AQI past 400, briefly the worst air of any major city on Earth.

items.title: The Camp Fire destroyed a town called Paradise in a single morning
items.description: California's deadliest wildfire killed 85 people and burned 18,000 buildings in 2018, moving at times faster than residents could drive away.

items.title: Maui 2023: the deadliest US wildfire in over a century
items.description: Wind-driven flames destroyed the historic town of Lahaina in hours, killing at least 100 people on an island few associated with wildfire risk.

items.title: The 2010 Russian fires doubled Moscow's death rate for weeks
items.description: Record heat ignited peat and forest fires whose smog halved visibility in the capital; the heatwave-and-smoke combination killed an estimated 56,000 people.

items.title: The Dust Bowl: when ploughed-up prairie buried whole towns in dust
items.description: In the 1930s, drought plus destroyed grassland produced 'black blizzards' that darkened skies as far as New York and displaced 2.5 million Americans - the classic warning about soil.

items.title: 12 million hectares of productive land are lost to desertification every year
items.description: Overgrazing, deforestation and climate change are degrading drylands that feed 2 billion people - Central Asia is among the regions most exposed.

items.title: Africa is building an 8,000-km wall of trees across the whole continent
items.description: The Great Green Wall aims to restore 100 million hectares of degraded land from Senegal to Djibouti - one of the most ambitious restoration projects ever attempted.

items.title: Scientists say we are living through the sixth mass extinction
items.description: Species are vanishing tens to hundreds of times faster than the natural background rate - the only mass extinction in Earth's history caused by a single species.

items.title: The passenger pigeon went from 3 billion birds to zero in 50 years
items.description: Once so numerous their flocks darkened American skies for hours, the species was hunted to extinction; the last bird, Martha, died in a zoo in 1914.

items.title: The dodo: extinct within a century of humans finding its island
items.description: Flightless and fearless, the dodo of Mauritius was gone by 1681 - the first extinction widely recognised as caused by people, and now the very symbol of it.

items.title: The western black rhino was declared extinct in 2011
items.description: Poached for horn worth more than gold by weight, the subspecies was hunted from thousands of animals to none in a few decades.

items.title: The Yangtze river dolphin was the first dolphin humans drove extinct
items.description: The baiji survived 20 million years of Earth's changes but not 50 years of dams, boat traffic and fishing nets; it was declared functionally extinct in 2006.

items.title: Fewer than 10 vaquita porpoises may remain on Earth
items.description: The world's smallest and rarest marine mammal is drowning in gillnets set illegally for another endangered fish whose swim bladder sells for thousands of dollars.

items.title: The 'insect apocalypse': flying insect numbers fell 75% in some regions
items.description: Long-term studies in German nature reserves found three-quarters of flying insect biomass gone in under 30 years - and insects pollinate a third of our food.

items.title: When the bees started disappearing: colony collapse disorder
items.description: Beginning in 2006, beekeepers found hives suddenly empty of workers; the mystery focused the world's attention on pesticides, parasites and pollinator decline.

items.title: A third of the world's fish stocks are being fished beyond their limits
items.description: Industrial fleets remove wildlife from the sea faster than it can reproduce; some famous fisheries, like Newfoundland's cod, collapsed and never came back.

items.title: Commercial whaling killed nearly 3 million whales in the 20th century
items.description: Some species were reduced by over 99% before the 1986 moratorium; blue whales - the largest animals ever to exist - are still recovering.

items.title: Ivory: 100,000 elephants were killed in just three years
items.description: Between 2010 and 2012 poachers killed roughly one African elephant every 15 minutes; ivory bans and enforcement have since slowed - not stopped - the killing.

items.title: The pangolin: the world's most trafficked mammal is one most people can't name
items.description: More than a million pangolins were poached in a decade for scales and meat - more than rhinos, elephants and tigers combined.

items.title: CO₂ in the air is the highest it has been in at least 3 million years
items.description: Measurements at Mauna Loa - the famous Keeling Curve - passed 420 ppm; the last time levels were this high, seas were metres higher and trees grew near the South Pole.

items.title: The Keeling Curve: the single graph that proved the atmosphere is changing
items.description: Started in 1958 on a Hawaiian volcano, one scientist's stubborn daily CO₂ measurements became the most important environmental dataset ever collected.

items.title: The Paris Agreement: 195 nations signed the same climate promise
items.description: The 2015 treaty committed nearly every country to keep warming well below 2°C - the question that defines this century is whether they will deliver it.

items.title: The Montreal Protocol: the environmental treaty that actually worked
items.description: The 1987 ban on CFCs is healing the ozone hole, which is on track to fully recover this century - proof that global environmental action can succeed.

items.title: The ozone hole over Antarctica grew larger than North America
items.description: Discovered in 1985, the hole shocked the world into the fastest global treaty ever agreed; it remains the classic case of science catching a planetary emergency in time.

items.title: Kyoto, 1997: the first time the world put legal limits on greenhouse gases
items.description: The Kyoto Protocol bound industrialised countries to emission targets for the first time - imperfect and incomplete, but the foundation everything since was built on.

items.title: Thousands of scientists, one verdict: the IPCC's climate reports
items.description: The UN's climate panel reviews the entire body of climate science every few years; its 2023 report called this decade the decisive one for limiting warming.

items.title: Seas are rising at the fastest rate in 3,000 years
items.description: Global sea level has climbed about 20 cm since 1900 and the rise is accelerating - hundreds of millions of people live on land that could flood regularly by 2100.

items.title: The Arctic has lost about 40% of its summer sea ice since 1979
items.description: Satellites show September ice shrinking decade after decade; an effectively ice-free Arctic summer is now expected within most readers' lifetimes.

items.title: A quarter of the northern hemisphere's land is frozen - and it's thawing
items.description: Permafrost holds roughly twice as much carbon as the entire atmosphere; as it thaws it buckles roads, topples buildings and releases ancient methane.

items.title: Thwaites: the Antarctic glacier nicknamed 'Doomsday'
items.description: This Florida-sized glacier alone holds back enough ice to raise seas by 65 cm, and warm water is eating its underside faster than expected.

items.title: Greenland is losing about 270 billion tonnes of ice every year
items.description: The ice sheet's melt now contributes more to sea-level rise than Antarctica's; its complete loss - over centuries - would raise oceans by 7 metres.

items.title: Europe's 2003 heatwave killed more than 70,000 people
items.description: The summer that shocked Europe into taking heat seriously - and heatwaves of that scale are now many times more likely than they were then.

items.title: 2022: a third of Pakistan was underwater at once
items.description: Monsoon floods supercharged by record heat displaced 33 million people and did $30+ billion in damage - one of the costliest climate disasters ever recorded.

items.title: Hurricane Katrina drowned a major American city
items.description: The 2005 storm breached New Orleans' levees, killed more than 1,300 people and remains the costliest US natural disaster - a warning about infrastructure and rising seas.

items.title: Typhoon Haiyan's winds were the strongest ever recorded at landfall
items.description: The 2013 super typhoon flattened entire Philippine cities with 300+ km/h gusts and a storm surge like a tsunami, killing over 6,000 people.

items.title: Cyclone Nargis: 138,000 dead in the Irrawaddy Delta
items.description: The 2008 storm surge swept tens of kilometres inland over Myanmar's low-lying delta - a catastrophe deepened by the loss of protective mangrove forests.

items.title: Cities can be 10°C hotter than the countryside around them
items.description: Asphalt and concrete store the day's heat and release it all night; the 'urban heat island' effect turns ordinary heatwaves deadly in dense city centres.

items.title: The world generates over 60 million tonnes of e-waste a year - and rising
items.description: Discarded phones, TVs and computers are the fastest-growing waste stream on Earth; less than a quarter is formally recycled, despite containing gold, copper and rare metals.

items.title: Agbogbloshie: where the world's electronics went to die
items.description: The Ghanaian scrapyard became a symbol of the e-waste trade - workers burning cables for copper in smoke thick with lead and dioxins.

items.title: Fast fashion: a garbage truck of clothes is dumped or burned every second
items.description: Clothing production doubled while the time we keep each garment halved; textiles now rival aviation in emissions and shed microplastics with every wash.

items.title: A third of humanity can no longer see the Milky Way
items.description: Skyglow from cities has erased the night sky for billions of people - and disrupts the navigation, breeding and migration of countless nocturnal species.

items.title: Noise pollution is shortening lives - the WHO calls it an underestimated threat
items.description: Chronic traffic noise raises blood pressure and heart-attack risk; in Europe alone it is linked to thousands of premature deaths every year.

items.title: There are over 100 million pieces of debris orbiting Earth right now
items.description: Dead satellites and fragments circle the planet at 28,000 km/h; a cascade of collisions - the Kessler syndrome - could make some orbits unusable for generations.

items.title: Fertiliser runoff is turning lakes green across the planet
items.description: Nutrient pollution feeds toxic algal blooms - like the one that shut down the water supply of half a million people in Toledo, Ohio in 2014.

items.title: Salt is quietly ruining a fifth of the world's irrigated farmland
items.description: Poor drainage leaves salt behind with every watering - the same process that degraded Central Asia's cotton lands - taking millions of hectares out of production.

items.title: In Appalachia, mining doesn't dig into mountains - it removes them
items.description: Mountaintop-removal coal mining has flattened some 500 peaks and buried over 3,000 km of streams under rubble in the eastern United States.

items.title: Coal is still the single largest source of energy-related CO₂
items.description: From mine to smokestack, coal's footprint spans acid drainage, ash spills, mercury and about 40% of global fossil-fuel CO₂ - which is why its phase-out dominates climate policy.

items.title: Solar power became the cheapest electricity in history
items.description: The International Energy Agency's 2020 outlook declared solar the lowest-cost electricity ever seen; global capacity has been doubling roughly every three years.

items.title: Wind turbines now power hundreds of millions of homes
items.description: Wind has grown from a niche technology to over 1,000 GW installed worldwide - in several countries it is already the biggest single source of electricity.

items.title: Electric cars went from golf carts to one in five new cars sold
items.description: Global EV sales passed 17 million a year - the fastest energy-technology shift since the smartphone, and the main hope for cutting city air pollution from traffic.

items.title: Renewables now supply about 30% of the world's electricity
items.description: Hydro, wind and solar together crossed the 30% threshold - the milestone analysts call the beginning of the end of the fossil-fuel power era.

items.title: The Anthropocene: scientists debate naming a whole epoch after us
items.description: Human impact - from plastics in sediment to radioactive fallout - is now so deep in the geological record that geologists formally considered declaring a new epoch of Earth history.

items.title: Earth Overshoot Day: humanity uses a year of resources by mid-year
items.description: Each year the date when consumption exceeds what Earth can regenerate arrives earlier - running the planet like a bank account permanently in overdraft.

items.title: Half the world's wetlands disappeared in the 20th century
items.description: Drained for farms and cities, the marshes that filter water, buffer floods and shelter migratory birds vanished faster than any other ecosystem type.

items.title: Mangroves are worth billions - and we cut down a third of them
items.description: These coastal forests absorb storm surges, store more carbon than rainforest and nurse fish stocks, yet vast belts were cleared for shrimp farms and development.

items.title: One in six deaths worldwide is linked to pollution of some kind
items.description: The Lancet Commission on pollution and health found pollution kills around 9 million people a year - triple the toll of AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria combined.
